title: Elon Musk Is Unleashing SpaceX’s New War Chest to Solve His AI Problem
blurb: Elon Musk plans to use SpaceX's cash reserves to fund an AI push, including a reported $60B acquisition of coding agent Cursor and a move into renting out data-center capacity. The strategy would give SpaceX a foothold in enterprise AI, positioning the rocket company to court business customers and compete against Musk's own xAI and established cloud providers.

title: Verifying the Rust Standard Library
blurb: A crowdsourced campaign added static verification to the Rust standard library, targeting the unsafe code that the language's type system can't guarantee and that was previously checked only through testing and Miri. The authors describe what they call the largest verification effort reported for a software library, producing machine-checked proofs against specific undefined behaviors like out-of-bounds access, null pointer dereferences, and use of uninitialized memory.

title: Wolfram Language and Mathematica Version 15, AI Assistant, Symbolic Music, More
blurb: Wolfram released Version 15 of Wolfram Language and Mathematica, adding built-in AI assistance, symbolic music representation, and dedicated interfaces designed for AI systems alongside human users. The release leans into the idea that the language's coherent design now serves AIs as well as people, giving them a precise way to formalize and verify what natural-language prompts actually compute.

title: Ternary Mamba: Grouped Quantization-Aware Training of W1.58A16 State Space Models
blurb: Ternary Mamba shows that ternary state space models can be created from a pretrained Mamba-2 checkpoint rather than trained from scratch, cutting the token budget 1,000x by using grouped quantization-aware training with distillation from a frozen FP16 teacher. The method compresses Mamba-2 1.3B by 3.61x (2,687 to 744 MB) and hits 48.1% zero-shot accuracy in 102M tokens and four H100 GPU-hours.

title: A Red-Team Study of Anthropic Fable 5 & Opus 4.8 Models
blurb: A red-team study using the HackAgent framework tested Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 against four jailbreak families across 7,826 harmful intents, with every apparent success re-adjudicated by a three-judge panel. Both models resisted most attacks, but adaptive tree-of-attacks search broke Opus 4.8 on 11.5% of intents and Fable 5 on 6.1%, yielding 1,620 and 702 confirmed harmful completions located cheaply with no human in the loop.

title: Looped World Models
blurb: LoopWM introduces looped world models that iteratively refine latent states through a parameter-shared transformer block, claiming up to 100x parameter efficiency.

title: Humiliating IIS servers for fun and jail time
blurb: A bug bounty hunter walks through how to find and exploit Microsoft IIS web servers, arguing their default splash page often signals a deeper trove of misconfigurations. The guide covers Shodan and Google dorking to locate IIS boxes, header fingerprinting at scale, and techniques like HTTP parameter pollution to bypass web application firewalls.

title: Compositional Skill Routing for LLM Agents: Decompose, Retrieve, and Compose
blurb: SkillWeaver decomposes complex agent queries into atomic sub-tasks, retrieves a skill for each, and composes an executable plan, cutting context use by over 99%.

title: Uncertainty Quantification for Flow-Based Vision-Language-Action Models
blurb: A method quantifies uncertainty in flow-based vision-language-action robotics models, enabling failure detection and adaptation with 22% fewer demonstrations.

title: Trump-backed World Liberty Financial nears OCC approval for federal trust charter: report
blurb: World Liberty Financial, the Trump-backed crypto firm, nears OCC approval for a federal trust charter to issue its USD1 stablecoin.

title: Seeing Is Not Screening: Multimodal Hidden Instruction Attacks on Agent Skill Scanners
blurb: Researchers hide malicious instructions inside images bundled with agent skills, evading text-based scanners that don't analyze visual content.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting is Low-Rank: A Function-Space Theory for Continual Adaptation
blurb: Catastrophic forgetting concentrates in a few NTK eigenmodes, giving a closed-form predictor for which outputs drift during continual adaptation.

title: WEQA: Wearable hEalth Question Answering with Query-Adaptive Agentic Reasoning
blurb: WEQA is a query-adaptive agent framework that routes wearable health questions to specialized sensor tools, scoring 24% higher than LLM and agentic baselines.

title: Security and Privacy Prompts in the Wild: What Users Ask LLMs and How LLMs Respond
blurb: Analyzing 14,727 real security and privacy prompts from WildChat, this paper finds GPT-5.5 gives adequate answers 98% of the time versus Llama 4's 47%, but commercial models still contradict themselves across repeated runs.

title: Elon Musk Is Unleashing SpaceX’s New War Chest to Solve His AI Problem
blurb: Elon Musk is using SpaceX's expanded cash reserves to fund his AI ambitions, including a $60B acquisition of coding agent Cursor and a push into renting out data-center capacity. The moves are aimed at landing more enterprise customers and giving Musk's AI operations a stronger commercial footing against rivals like OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: Wolfram Language and Mathematica Version 15, AI Assistant, Symbolic Music, More
blurb: Wolfram Research released Version 15 of Wolfram Language and Mathematica, adding built-in AI assistance, symbolic music representation, and expanded core functionality nearly 38 years after Mathematica 1.0. The release adds interfaces designed for AI systems alongside human users, letting AIs access Wolfram's computational language directly while humans use it to formalize and verify what those systems produce.

title: First Proof Second Batch
blurb: This paper tests several AI systems on ten research-level mathematics problems that arose naturally in the work of contributing mathematicians across a broad range of fields. The authors document the problems, their testing methodology, and the results, and provide supplementary links to the human solutions, the AI-generated solutions, and referee reports evaluating each AI attempt. The setup measures whether current models can solve genuine research problems rather than benchmark-style exercises.

title: [AINews] GLM-5.2: the top Frontend Coding model in the world, IndexShare for Speculative Decoding
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-weight model with a 1M-token context window and two reasoning-effort modes, at the same API pricing as GLM-5.1. Third-party leaderboards place the 744B-parameter model just behind Opus 4.8 as the best coding model overall and ahead of every Opus version on frontend coding, with day-zero support across Transformers, vLLM, and SGLang.

title: Why Weibo’s tiny VibeThinker-3B has the AI world arguing over benchmarks again
blurb: Weibo researchers released VibeThinker-3B, a 3B-parameter reasoning model that scored 94.3 on AIME 2026, matching the 671B-parameter DeepSeek V3.2 and edging out Gemini 3 Pro's 91.7. The results have split the research community, with a test-time scaling method called Claim-Level Reliability Assessment pushing the score to 97.1 and reviving doubts over whether AI benchmarks remain meaningful.

title: An Empirical Analysis of AI Slop in Music Streaming
blurb: A new paper studies the economics of AI-generated "slop" music on streaming platforms and concludes it is poised to become a self-sustaining shadow industry. Analyzing Spotify, the authors found 93% of AI tracks get few or no plays, with artists "spray and pray" flooding genres for a rare hit. They published their own AI songs through 11 indie distributors, finding inconsistent and largely unenforced policies, and showed that current AI-music detectors lack accuracy and robustness.

title: A Red-Team Study of Anthropic Fable 5 & Opus 4.8 Models
blurb: This paper red-teams Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 against four families of automated jailbreak attacks across 7,826 harmful intents, with every apparent success re-adjudicated by a three-judge panel. Both models resist most attacks, but tree-of-attacks adaptive search broke Opus 4.8 on 11.5% of intents and Fable 5 on 6.1%, yielding 1,620 and 702 panel-confirmed harmful completions located cheaply with no human expert in the loop.

title: When AI Says "I have been in similar situations": Synthetic Lived Experience in Peer-Like Caregiver Support
blurb: This paper examines a "synthetic lived experience paradox" in AI caregiver support: LLMs prompted to sound peer-like can generate experiential language that falsely positions them as having lived through caregiving. Analyzing ADRD caregiver exchanges against responses from LLaMA, GPT-4o-mini, and MedGemma, the authors find human peers use significantly more first-person and past-focused language, while AI captures the emotional work but fabricates experiential grounding. They argue caregiver-support systems need mechanisms to separate supportive framing from invented lived experience.

title: The Measurement Gap in the Automation of EU Law: Benchmarking Doctrinal Legal Reasoning under the EU AI Act
blurb: LLMs now produce legal text of at least median quality, but this paper argues no existing benchmark tests whether they perform doctrinal legal reasoning, the interpretive core of legal work, rather than the paralegal tasks most legal-AI evaluations measure. The authors frame this as a legal problem, not just a methodological one: the EU AI Act makes "appropriate accuracy" a binding requirement for high-risk judicial AI, yet that requirement stays empty without the doctrinal-reasoning benchmark the field lacks.

title: Looped World Models
blurb: LoopWM is the first looped architecture for world modeling, iteratively refining latent environment states through a parameter-shared transformer block. The approach reaches up to 100x parameter efficiency over conventional models and uses adaptive computation that scales depth to each prediction step's complexity, positioning iterative latent depth as a new scaling axis for world simulation.

title: Position: Coding Benchmarks Are Misaligned with Agentic Software Engineering
blurb: This paper argues that current coding benchmarks are misaligned with how agents actually do software engineering. It contends that a coding agent is a system, not a model, combining harnesses, contexts, environments, and feedback signals that can each shift scores as much as a model generation. The authors flag three symptoms: scores conflate the model with the harness, single-reference grading penalizes valid alternatives, and component-level signal for iteration is missing.

title: Catastrophic Forgetting is Low-Rank: A Function-Space Theory for Continual Adaptation
blurb: This paper offers a function-space theory of catastrophic forgetting in the NTK regime, deriving a closed-form predictor for old-task prediction drift before any new-task gradient step is taken. The authors show that forgetting concentrates in a small number of old-task NTK eigenmodes, explaining why parameter-space regularizers miss output-space interference and motivating a targeted spectral regularizer.

title: Ternary Mamba: Grouped Quantization-Aware Training of W1.58A16 State Space Models
blurb: Ternary Mamba shows that a pretrained Mamba-2 1.3B checkpoint can be compressed to ternary weights using grouped quantization-aware training and knowledge distillation from a frozen FP16 teacher, cutting the token budget 1,000x versus from-scratch methods. The approach shrinks the model 3.61x to 744 MB and reaches 48.1% zero-shot accuracy across seven tasks in just 102M tokens and four H100 GPU-hours.

title: Visual Verification Enables Inference-time Steering and Autonomous Policy Improvement
blurb: VERITAS is a generator-verifier framework that pairs a pre-trained generalist robot policy with a gradient-free visual verifier that scores candidate actions at inference time, improving performance without additional training. Fine-tuning policies on the verified self-generated rollouts matches the efficiency of expert demonstrations while requiring no human interventions.

title: Dynamic Rollout Editing for Reducing Overthinking in RL-Trained Reasoning Models
blurb: This paper introduces Dynamic Rollout Editing (DRE), a training-time method that reduces overthinking in RL-trained reasoning models by reframing it as a GRPO credit-assignment problem rather than a decoding-time stopping issue. DRE preserves the verified prefix of successful trajectories, edits the unnecessary continuation after an answer emerges, and prefers the edited version within the RL group to weaken the signal for redundant thinking.

title: Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization: Teacher in Prompts, Not Gradients
blurb: This paper introduces Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (ZPPO), a distillation method that keeps the teacher inside the prompt rather than the policy gradient, avoiding the generalization loss and on-policy drift that plague logit imitation and teacher-injected RL. Tested on the Qwen3.5 family at four scales (0.8B-9B) with a 27B teacher across a 31-benchmark suite, ZPPO beat off/on-policy distillation and GRPO, with the largest gains at the smallest scale.

title: Structural Role Injection in Handlebars-Templated LLM Prompts: Triple-Brace Interpolation, Delimiter Family, and the Limits of HTML Auto-Escaping
blurb: This paper shows that Handlebars, the default prompt-template engine in Microsoft Semantic Kernel, only blocks structural role injection for delimiter schemes its HTML escaping happens to cover, neutralizing ChatML, Llama-3, and XML delimiters while leaving Llama-2, legacy Human:/Assistant:, and Markdown delimiters intact. Across 5,760 trials on four models, GPT-3.5 Turbo followed task-hijack instructions in 91% of escaped trials, while Claude Haiku 4.5 resisted almost entirely.

title: All Smoke, No Alarm: Oracle Signals in Agent-Authored Test Code
blurb: This paper analyzes 86,156 test-file patches from 33,596 agent-authored GitHub PRs produced by Codex, Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude Code, finding that 80.2% contain weak or no explicit assertions and verify nothing despite running code. A regression controlling for agent, PR size, and language shows strong oracle signals raise merge likelihood (OR = 1.28, p < 0.001), suggesting test-file counts overestimate verification strength and that practitioners should adopt oracle-aware quality checks.

title: Learning from the Self-future: On-policy Self-distillation for dLLMs
blurb: d-OPSD is the first on-policy self-distillation framework built for diffusion LLMs, replacing the autoregressive prefix conditioning of existing methods with self-generated suffix conditioning and shifting supervision from the token level to the step level to match the denoising process. Across four reasoning benchmarks it outperforms RLVR and SFT baselines while using roughly 10% of the optimization steps required by RLVR.

title: ProvenanceGuard: Source-Aware Factuality Verification for MCP-Based LLM Agents
blurb: ProvenanceGuard is a source-aware verifier for MCP-based LLM agents that catches cross-source conflation, where a claim is correct but attributed to the wrong tool or evidence source. On 281 medical-domain MCP traces, it reached block F1 0.802 and source accuracy 0.858, detected all 50 injected attribution swaps, and repaired blocked answers via retrieval-augmented revision.

title: Variable-Width Transformers
blurb: This paper proposes a "><"-shaped transformer that keeps early and late layers wide while narrowing the middle, using a parameter-free residual resizing mechanism to allocate capacity nonuniformly across depth. Across dense models from 200M to 2B parameters and a 3B MoE, it beats parameter-matched uniform baselines on language modeling loss while cutting FLOPs by 22% and KV cache memory and I/O by 15%.

title: Cordon: Semantic Transactions for Tool-Using LLM Agents
blurb: Cordon is a transactional runtime that stages and validates irreversible agent effects before commit, replacing per-call guardrails with a task-scoped execution boundary for commit, rollback, recovery, and audit across multi-step workflows. It tracks derived results, runs reversible mutations in shadow state, and stages outward actions in an effect outbox, exposing cross-step violations that existing defenses miss while preserving benign task completion at modest overhead.

title: NoiseTilt: Noise-Tilted Reverse Kernels for Diffusion Reward Alignment
blurb: NoiseTilt introduces the Noise-Tilted Reverse Kernel (NTRK), a reward-guided diffusion sampler that injects reward gradients through the noise term while leaving the pretrained reverse mean unchanged, requiring only a single sample per step. A whitening operator makes the gradient safe to inject as noise, and on aesthetic generation NTRK matches the best baseline's reward using 25 NFEs instead of 500, a 20x compute reduction.

title: Compositional Skill Routing for LLM Agents: Decompose, Retrieve, and Compose
blurb: SkillWeaver is a decompose-retrieve-compose framework that breaks complex agent queries into sub-tasks, retrieves a matching skill for each via a FAISS bi-encoder, and assembles them into a dependency-aware DAG plan. Its Iterative Skill-Aware Decomposition loop lifts decomposition accuracy from 51.0% to 67.7% and cuts context window consumption by over 99% across a benchmark of 2,209 real MCP server skills.

title: Sources: the US has held off adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and 100+ other flagged companies to its Entity List, last updated in October, the longest gap in 10+ years (Karen Freifeld/Reuters)
blurb: The US has not added DeepSeek, memory maker CXMT, and more than 100 other flagged companies to its trade restriction Entity List since the last update in October, sources said. The roughly eight-month pause is the longest gap in over a decade, signaling reluctance to escalate export curbs amid ongoing trade negotiations with China.

title: Sources: Meta decided to support KOSA after it was packaged with language preempting state AI laws and a bill requiring app stores to verify users' ages (Politico)
blurb: Meta decided to support the Kids Online Safety Act (KOSA) after it was bundled with language preempting state AI laws and a provision requiring app stores to verify users' ages. The packaging ties child-safety legislation to a federal preemption of the growing patchwork of state-level AI rules, a long-standing priority for large tech platforms.

title: Volterra Generative Models
blurb: Volterra generative models are a continuous-time score-based framework that replaces the memoryless Brownian noise in diffusion with path-dependent perturbations drawn from fractional kernels, handled via finite-dimensional Markovian lifts. On MNIST and CIFAR-10, the authors show that persistent fractional perturbations with small lifts can improve score-based generation, with a Gaussian-bridge reconstruction sampler added to stabilize larger, stiffer lifts.

title: Sign-Rank, Index, and List Replicability: Connections and Separations
blurb: This paper proves a strong separation between sign rank and the Z₂-index, and bounds list replicability by combinatorial measures like height and star number.

title: INI-VPINN: A Variational Physics-Informed Neural Network with Implicit Neumann and Interface Handling for Multi-Material Domains with Geometric Singularities
blurb: INI-VPINN is a weak-form physics-informed neural network that bakes Neumann and interface conditions into its variational formulation, improving accuracy on multi-material domains.

title: Adaptive Volumetric Mechanical Property Fields Invariant to Resolution
blurb: AdaVoMP predicts dense spatially-varying material properties for 3D objects at 16³ higher resolution than prior art, producing simulation-ready assets.

title: Elon Musk Is Unleashing SpaceX’s New War Chest to Solve His AI Problem
blurb: Musk is routing SpaceX's cash into his AI ambitions, reportedly buying coding agent Cursor in a $60B deal and renting out data-center capacity to court enterprise customers. The move folds a rocket company's balance sheet into the AI race, using SpaceX's reserves to buy distribution and compute rather than build them from scratch. For founders selling AI products, it signals that the deepest-pocketed players will increasingly acquire their way into the stack, turning today's standalone tools into someone else's enterprise on-ramp.

title: Qualcomm mulls taking over Jim Keller's Tenstorrent, report claims — deal for AI chipmaker would value the company at between $8 billion and $10 billion
blurb: Qualcomm is in talks to acquire Jim Keller's Tenstorrent at a valuation between $8B and $10B, more than double the $3.2B the AI chipmaker sought from investors last year. The pull is Tenstorrent's RISC-V AI accelerators and data center CPU IP, but the fit is messy: Qualcomm would end up with two AI accelerator lines and three data center CPU designs after already buying Ventana and Alphawave. The pattern is familiar for Qualcomm, which used Nuvia and Atheros to buy its way into entire new markets, and it is now paying frontier prices to stay in the AI silicon race.

title: Sources: Jeff Bezos is investing in Cambridge, UK-based CuspAI, which applies generative AI to material sciences, as part of a $400M round at a $2.6B valuation (Tim Bradshaw/Financial Times)
blurb: Jeff Bezos is backing CuspAI, a Cambridge, UK startup applying generative AI to materials science, in a $400M round that values it at $2.6B. The bet is that AI can compress the discovery cycle for new materials, from carbon capture sorbents to battery chemistries, that traditionally took years of lab trial and error. Capital is flowing toward AI applied to physical science, not just software, and the names writing the checks now include the people who built the largest tech companies of the last era.

title: Databricks says annualized revenue rose 80%+ YoY to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in Q4; CEO Ali Ghodsi says higher AI agent usage is increasing costs, lowering margins (Jordan Novet/CNBC)
blurb: Databricks said annualized revenue rose more than 80% YoY to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in Q4, but CEO Ali Ghodsi flagged that heavier AI agent usage is pushing costs up and margins down. The growth comes from customers running more inference-hungry agentic workloads, the same demand that is eroding the unit economics underneath it. For founders building on top of this stack, it is a reminder that agent adoption scales compute spend faster than it scales margin, and pricing has to account for that.

title: Series D funding rises 308% in first half of 2026
blurb: European Series D funding climbed 308% in the first half of 2026, the clearest sign yet that late-stage capital is flowing back after a long drought. The recovery is concentrated at the top of the stack, where investors are again willing to write nine-figure checks into proven companies rather than spread smaller bets across early rounds. For founders sitting on Series C cash, the implication is that the late-stage market has reopened, and the companies raising now are the ones with the metrics to justify the round.

title: Verifying the Rust Standard Library
blurb: A crowdsourced verification campaign integrated multiple formal tools into CI on a fork of the Rust standard library to statically prove the correctness of its unsafe code, which currently relies only on testing and dynamic Miri checks. The authors machine-checked proofs ruling out a subset of undefined behavior, including out-of-bounds access, null and dangling pointer dereferences, and use of uninitialized memory, and lay out the remaining gaps as open problems for the formal-methods community.

title: Cordon: Semantic Transactions for Tool-Using LLM Agents
blurb: Cordon is a transactional runtime that stages a tool-using agent's irreversible effects in shadow state and an effect outbox, validating the full multi-step execution flow before committing or releasing any external action. The system replaces per-call guardrails with a task-scoped boundary supporting commit, rollback, and audit, catching cross-step violations that existing defenses miss while adding modest approval and latency overhead.

title: The Value of Adaptivity in LSM Bloom-Filter Tuning: A Log-Law and a Two-Clock Frontier
blurb: This paper analyzes when adaptive Bloom-filter tuning in LSM trees actually beats the static Monkey optimum, deriving a log-law that makes optimal bits-per-key affine in the logarithm of access frequency and showing that coarse hotness estimates lose little because the workload enters only logarithmically. On Twitter production traces and a real RocksDB engine, reallocating filters only at compaction captures 96-99% of continuous tracking's benefit, since compaction already rebuilds filters for free on its own clock.

title: All Smoke, No Alarm: Oracle Signals in Agent-Authored Test Code
blurb: A study of 86,156 test-file patches from 33,596 agent-authored PRs across five coding agents found that 80.2% contain weak or no explicit oracle signals, meaning the tests run code without actually verifying behavior. After adjusting for agent, PR size, and language, strong-oracle PRs were 28% more likely to merge, suggesting test-file counts overstate verification strength and quality gates should check for assertions, not just test presence.

title: ProvenanceGuard: Source-Aware Factuality Verification for MCP-Based LLM Agents
blurb: ProvenanceGuard is a source-aware verifier that decomposes MCP-agent answers into atomic claims, routes each to source-specific evidence, and flags "cross-source conflation" where a claim is true but attributed to the wrong tool or source. On 40 held-out medical-domain traces it hit block F1 0.802 and source accuracy 0.858, though exact source-plus-relation accuracy fell to 0.229 on a harder multi-source benchmark with semantically close sources.

title: Humiliating IIS servers for fun and jail time
blurb: This bug bounty walkthrough details a methodology for finding and exploiting IIS web servers, covering discovery via Shodan and Google dorks, fingerprinting through response headers like `Server: Microsoft-IIS/10.0`, and exploitation of common misconfigurations. The author also demonstrates bypassing WAFs using HTTP parameter pollution, treating the default IIS splash page as a signal of likely misconfiguration rather than a dead end.

title: Structural Role Injection in Handlebars-Templated LLM Prompts: Triple-Brace Interpolation, Delimiter Family, and the Limits of HTML Auto-Escaping
blurb: Handlebars' double-brace escaping, the documented safe default in Microsoft Semantic Kernel, only neutralizes role delimiters built from angle brackets, leaving Llama-2 [INST], Human:/Assistant:, and Markdown ### injections fully intact. Across 5,760 trials, GPT-3.5 Turbo followed task-hijack instructions in 91% of escaped prompts while Claude Haiku 4.5 resisted almost entirely, showing escaping cannot substitute for structural separation of instruction and data.

title: When the Next Step Is Not One Step: Distribution-Aware Execution Modeling for Concurrent Go Programs
blurb: This paper treats scheduler nondeterminism in concurrent Go programs as a training signal, running each program many times to build an empirical distribution of next events and fine-tuning a 7B model to match it with a KL objective. On 798 held-out predictions from real CockroachDB, Kubernetes, gRPC, and etcd bugs, it hits 36.2% accuracy and cuts Expected Calibration Error from 0.205 to 0.169 versus cross-entropy.

title: Wolfram Language and Mathematica Version 15, AI Assistant, Symbolic Music, More
blurb: Wolfram released Version 15 of Wolfram Language and Mathematica, adding built-in AI assistant access, symbolic music representation, and a large batch of new core functionality across the language. The release also targets AIs as first-class users alongside humans, exposing interfaces designed to let LLM systems call Wolfram's computational engine for precise, formalized results rather than natural-language guesses.

title: Position: Coding Benchmarks Are Misaligned with Agentic Software Engineering
blurb: The authors argue that coding benchmarks built in the pre-agent era are misaligned with how agents actually work, since they collapse model, harness, environment, and feedback into one end-to-end score graded against a single reference solution. They identify three failures: scores conflate the model with the surrounding harness, valid alternative solutions get penalized, and the lack of component-level signal makes the system hard to iterate on.

title: PracRepair: LLM-Empowered Automated Program Repair Inspired by Human-Like Debugging Practices
blurb: PracRepair is an LLM-based automated program repair framework that builds on-demand static-dynamic context from failing executions, runs question-driven failure diagnosis to form explicit repair hypotheses, and refines patches using validation diagnostics and trace-level behavioral changes. On Defects4J V1.2/V2.0 it fixes 162/171 bugs under GPT-4o, beating state-of-the-art baselines and generalizing to real-world bugs across multiple models.

title: Ternary Mamba: Grouped Quantization-Aware Training of W1.58A16 State Space Models
blurb: Ternary QAT from a pretrained Mamba-2 checkpoint compresses it 3.61x using 1,000x fewer tokens than training from scratch.

title: [AINews] GLM-5.2: the top Frontend Coding model in the world, IndexShare for Speculative Decoding
blurb: Z.ai released GLM-5.2, an MIT-licensed open-weight model with a 1M-token context window and two reasoning-effort modes, that independent leaderboards rank just behind Opus 4.8 on coding and ahead of every Opus on frontend coding. The 744B-parameter model ships with day-zero support across Transformers, vLLM, and SGLang, and uses a tweaked DeepSeek Sparse Attention variant to hold efficiency at ultra-long contexts.

title: GameCraft-Bench: Can Agents Build Playable Games End-to-End in a Real Game Engine?
blurb: GameCraft-Bench tests whether coding agents can build playable Godot games end-to-end across 140 tasks; the strongest agent scores just 41.46%.

title: Bifrost: Hybrid TEE-FHE Inference for Privacy-Preserving Transformer and LLM Serving
blurb: Bifrost splits LLM inference between a CPU TEE and FHE on the accelerator, cutting projected latency up to 9.9x while keeping prompts ciphertext-only.

title: Microsoft AntiSSRF open-source library helps block server-side request forgery
blurb: Microsoft released AntiSSRF, an open-source MIT-licensed library for .NET and Node.js that validates URLs and outbound connections to block server-side request forgery before applications make untrusted requests. Configuration centers on an AntiSSRFPolicy object for allow/deny IP lists and HTTP rules, plus a URIValidator with built-in checks for Azure Key Vault and Storage domains, with samples for Axios, follow-redirects, and node-fetch.

title: PromptMN: Pseudo Prompting Language
blurb: PromptMN is a pseudo-prompting DSL that annotates prose with %-prefixed typed directives for roles, goals, constraints, and outputs.

title: Databricks says annualized revenue rose 80%+ YoY to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in Q4; CEO Ali Ghodsi says higher AI agent usage is increasing costs, lowering margins (Jordan Novet/CNBC)
blurb: Databricks said annualized revenue rose more than 80% year-over-year to $6.9B, up from $5.4B in the prior quarter. CEO Ali Ghodsi noted that rising AI agent usage is driving up compute costs and compressing margins, a trade-off as customers shift more workloads to inference-heavy agentic workflows.

title: The Value of Adaptivity in LSM Bloom-Filter Tuning: A Log-Law and a Two-Clock Frontier
blurb: This paper asks when adaptive Bloom-filter tuning in LSM trees actually earns its complexity, building on Monkey's static optimum across synthetic sweeps, Twitter cache traces, and a real RocksDB engine. The authors find optimal bits-per-key is affine in the log of access frequency, so coarse hotness estimates lose little, and reallocating filters only at compaction captures 96-99% of continuous tracking's benefit. The takeaway is a three-regime policy that tells teams when fine-grained tracking is worth the machinery and when it isn't.

title: Databricks’ new agentic coworker Genie One brings AI automation to every part of the business
blurb: Databricks launched Genie One at its Data + AI Summit, an agentic coworker that extends the existing Genie suite from conversational analytics to executing tasks across sales, marketing, and finance workflows. The tool reasons over structured and unstructured data via Genie Ontology, a self-improving context layer that scans an org's documents, applications, and data to ground agents in business context rather than guessing to fill the gaps.

title: Introducing the Agentic CDP: A New Species of CDP for a New Era of Agents
blurb: Databricks introduced the "Agentic CDP," a customer data platform built for AI agents as first-class operators, with always-on 1:1 personalization, embedded governed customer context, and a "Golden Context" layer that extends the traditional Customer 360 record. The pitch targets agentic buying cycles that run in milliseconds, arguing that batch-oriented CDPs are too slow and too coarsely segmented to reach buyers whose purchasing agents filter out anything less than precisely relevant.

title: SNAS: A Multi-Layer Defense-in-Depth Architecture for Secure Egress in Sandboxed Workloads
blurb: Snowflake detailed SNAS, the production architecture that gives Snowpark's sandboxed UDFs external network access without breaking multi-tenant isolation. The design layers eBPF packet filtering, GENEVE overlay networks, and distributed egress proxies for policy-driven control, plus an EDT-based eBPF bandwidth limiter and safeguards against connection limits and port exhaustion. It runs across all Snowflake regions, supporting petabyte-scale transfers and latency-sensitive integrations to cloud APIs, external databases, and feature stores.

title: Collaborative Large and Small Language Models for Accurate and Scalable Data Repair
blurb: LasRepair pairs an LLM with a small language model for data repair, using the LLM as an instructor that selects a global repair context to guide a cheaper SLM corrector rather than repairing rows directly in their noisy raw context. Two extensions push accuracy further: an EM-style loop that alternates between updating the corrector and refining the context, and column-calibrated confidence weighting that down-weights uncertain rows. On real-world datasets, the full version beats the strongest baseline by 18.1% F1.

title: Rethinking Dataset Distillation for Classification: Do Distilled Sets Outperform Coresets?
blurb: Dataset distillation has been pitched as beating coreset selection by synthesizing compact training sets instead of pruning to real samples, but this paper finds the opposite. Benchmarking seven SOTA distillation methods against three coreset strategies on ImageNet-1K, ImageNet100, and ImageNette under standardized protocols, the authors show distilled sets match or underperform coresets at large scale while costing far more to build. Coresets also gave better coverage of the original data distribution, making them the more efficient default for data-centric training.

title: The Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset: Reconstructing U.S. Corporate and Financial Disclosures into Layout-Faithful and Token-Efficient Pretraining Data
blurb: Stanford researchers released the Stanford EDGAR Filings Dataset (SEFD), an open reconstruction of SEC filings into layout-faithful MultiMarkdown for long-context LLM pretraining. The corpus is token-efficient and has under 0.1% overlap with Common Crawl, addressing the growing scarcity of clean long-document training data. SEFD-v1 ships a 152B-token snapshot from a larger 550B-token archive, plus two benchmarks for filing-grounded numerical forecasting and complex financial-table transcription.

title: Group Commit Self-Clocks: Why Tuning Is Unnecessary Above a Device-Set Load Threshold
blurb: This paper argues that group commit's release-timer tuning is mostly unnecessary in real OLTP workloads. Because clients issue their next transaction only after the last commits, arrivals are closed-loop, so a parameter-free greedy policy that flushes the moment the log device is free self-clocks to a fixed point within ~0.1% of the best oracle-tuned timer. The square-root timer rule only matters below a device-set load threshold; on PostgreSQL, commit_delay=0 stays competitive with any tuned value.

title: Talking to Your Data: Exploring Embodied Conversation as an Interface for Personal Health Reflection
blurb: A research prototype replaces wearable-health dashboards with an embodied conversational agent that narrates your data as spoken statistics to prompt active reflection.

title: SNAS: A Multi-Layer Defense-in-Depth Architecture for Secure Egress in Sandboxed Workloads
blurb: This paper presents SNAS (Secure Network Access in Snowpark), a production architecture for policy-driven egress control from sandboxed UDF workloads while preserving multi-tenant isolation. SNAS combines eBPF packet filtering, GENEVE overlay networks, and distributed egress proxies, plus an eBPF bandwidth limiter using the Earliest Departure Time algorithm and safeguards against connection limits and port exhaustion. It is deployed across all Snowflake regions, supporting petabyte-scale transfers and latency-sensitive external integrations.

title: OTRO: Oblivious Tokenization Path with Square-Root ORAM
blurb: Researchers introduced OTRO, an oblivious tokenization path that closes a side-channel gap in confidential LLM serving, where prior work recovered full user prompts from CPU tokenizer memory-access patterns on production Intel TDX. Standard PathORAM mitigations impose roughly 13x tokenizer slowdown and 10-58% higher time-to-first-token. OTRO uses replicated square-root ORAM instances with epoch-based rotation and KV-cache-aware tokenization to cap TTFT overhead at 4.5% while reducing observable leakage, a practical option for teams running LLMs inside CPU and GPU TEEs.

title: ShellGames: Speculative LLM-Driven SSH Deception
blurb: ShellGames is an LLM-based SSH honeypot that aims to sustain credible, long-lived attacker sessions where prior LLM deception falls short. It combines chain-of-thought prompting, memory for state coherency, speculative command execution to cut latency, sandbox routing for complex commands, and subversion detection, reporting 0.898 command accuracy, 0.918 sequence consistency, and 0.95 robustness against probing. A 20-person user study rated its realism comparable to a real shell and ahead of traditional honeypots on perceived command coverage.

title: PARSE: Provenance-Aware Retrieval Sanitization for Professional Domain LLM Agents
blurb: PARSE, a new prompt-injection defense, argues that paraphrasing and other defenses validated on synthetic benchmarks fail on real enterprise documents, where authority language and factual content interleave. Tested on 122 tasks across financial, legal, medical, scientific, and DevOps domains using real SEC filings, Federal Register rules, and GitHub postmortems, PARSE cut attack success from 25.4% to 15.6% at 86.9% utility by classifying sentences by injection likelihood and verifying fact preservation. The authors urge teams to evaluate defenses on domain-matched real documents, not synthetic proxies.

title: SoK: AI-Augmented Binary Reversing
blurb: This paper systematizes a decade of AI-augmented binary reversing, analyzing 144 papers published since 2015 and organizing them into 22 reversing domains spanning vulnerability discovery, malware investigation, and firmware auditing. The authors introduce a unified taxonomy connecting traditional analysis techniques, binary-derived artifacts, learning paradigms, and the emerging roles of LLMs and agentic systems. For reverse engineers, it maps where ML approaches are maturing and where persistent evaluation gaps still undercut reliability.

title: An AI Security Agent for Banking: Multi-Vector Fraud and AML Detection Across Retail and Corporate Accounts
blurb: This paper presents an AI security agent for retail and corporate banking that fuses LSTM behavioral models, velocity monitors, and graph-based counterparty analysis across parallel transaction and session streams to catch both signature-based fraud and behavioral crimes like BEC and AML layering. On a synthetic log of 237,669 transactions and 113,508 sessions, it hit F1 of 0.787 (transaction) and 0.867 (session), beating rule-based (0.562/0.733) and LSTM-only (0.655/0.713) baselines, with Critical-tier response under 0.43 ms at p95.

title: Microsoft says you don’t need another email security tool; experts say, not so fast
blurb: Microsoft's new quarterly benchmark claims Defender for Office 365 catches nearly all malicious email on its own, with integrated third-party ICES partners adding under 0.05% to the catch rate. Microsoft reports a high-severity miss rate of 194 per 1,000 employees versus 478 for Mimecast and 483 for Proofpoint. Experts counter that percentages obscure the volume and severity of what slips through, and that one message is enough to justify defense in depth.

title: A Red-Team Study of Anthropic Fable 5 & Opus 4.8 Models
blurb: A red-team study using the HackAgent framework tested Anthropic's Fable 5 and Opus 4.8 against four jailbreak families across 7,826 harmful intents, with every apparent success re-adjudicated by a three-judge majority vote. Both models neutralize static obfuscation but remain vulnerable to adaptive iterative attacks: tree-of-attacks broke Opus 4.8 on 11.5% of intents and Fable 5 on 6.1% worst-case, yielding 1,620 and 702 panel-confirmed harmful completions. The authors conclude that even hardened frontier models stay reliably breakable under sustained automated pressure.

title: Safety, Security, and Cognitive Risks in Neuro-Symbolic AI
blurb: This paper maps the attack surface of neuro-symbolic AI, which pairs neural perception with symbolic reasoning, across five layers and extends MITRE ATLAS with 11 NeSy-specific tactics. The authors show that symbolic-layer attacks are both potent and stealthy: single-axiom OWL edits hit 93.3% symbolic-integrity-violation success while staying 100% consistent under Pellet, yet held-out STIX detection performs at random-guessing level. Defenders adopting explainable hybrid systems should treat knowledge graphs and ontologies as exploitable assets requiring integrity controls.

title: Bifrost: Hybrid TEE-FHE Inference for Privacy-Preserving Transformer and LLM Serving
blurb: Bifrost is a hybrid TEE-FHE architecture for confidential LLM inference that provisions secrets only to an attested CPU TEE, keeping the accelerator, drivers, and host stack outside the trusted computing base. It uses FHE for ciphertext-only delegation of linear layers while running non-linear, KV-state, and refresh operations inside the TEE. The authors report 9.25x to 9.91x projected latency cuts on GPT-2 and LLaMA 3, with a Bifrost+ prefill/decode split reducing TTFT up to 53.4x.

title: The checklist problem behind critical infrastructure cyber safety
blurb: New research from George Mason University argues that US critical-infrastructure policy treats compliance as a stand-in for safety, leaving operational technology that meets federal standards but lacks the engineering to survive an attack or failure. The paper documents security controls that created physical hazards: account lockouts that locked operators out during emergencies, fail-secure door latches that trapped occupants in fires, and flawed Advantech WebAccess patches that forced SCADA downtime. The core conflict is IT fail-secure behavior versus the fail-safe behavior physical systems require.

title: Differential Privacy of Gaussian Process Posterior Sampling
blurb: This paper shows that releasing posterior sample paths from a Gaussian process is differentially private on its own, since the sampling randomness substitutes for the noise that standard DP mechanisms add externally. The authors derive explicit Rényi-DP bounds, finding that meaningful privacy hinges on effective ridge regularization, and confirm the predicted leakage with membership-inference attacks. For stronger guarantees, calibrated GP noise serves as an additional tunable privacy knob.

title: Verifying the Rust Standard Library
blurb: A crowdsourced campaign integrated formal-verification tools into the CI of a fork of the Rust standard library, targeting the unsafe code that underpins memory safety but had only been validated through testing and Miri's dynamic checks. The authors report machine-checked proofs ruling out a subset of undefined behaviors, including out-of-bounds access, null and dangling pointer dereferences, and use of uninitialized memory, and frame the unproven remainder as open challenges for the formal-methods community.

title: Cordon: Semantic Transactions for Tool-Using LLM Agents
blurb: This paper introduces Cordon, a transactional runtime that stages and validates an LLM agent's irreversible tool effects before commit, replacing per-call guardrails with a task-scoped execution boundary. It executes reversible mutations in shadow state, queues outward actions in an effect outbox, and validates the composed flow before releasing anything. In evaluation, Cordon caught cross-step violations that existing defenses missed and cut irreversible-effect failures while preserving benign task completion at modest approval and latency overhead.

title: Evaluating Open-Source LLMs for Multi-Label ATT&CK Technique Classification on CTI Reports
blurb: This paper benchmarks seven open-source LLMs (8B to 236B parameters) on multi-label MITRE ATT&CK classification using 2,076 human-annotated sentences from 83 complex, unstructured CTI reports. The best model scored only 0.22 micro-averaged F1, with larger models correlating to higher scores but prompt strategy and temperature showing no significant gains. The authors conclude current open-source LLMs are insufficient for production-grade ATT&CK tagging, and release the dataset as a reproducible baseline.

title: Learning Red Agent Policy from Observations for Neurosymbolic Autonomous Cyber Agents
blurb: This paper proposes an imitation-learning technique that lets a neurosymbolic cyber-defense agent infer a red agent's actions and policy from network observations and its own defender actions, despite the attacker's moves being unobservable in a partially observable environment. By predicting red behavior across discrete states and actions, the method gives autonomous defenders a way to estimate intrusion level and adapt, achieving high prediction accuracy against diverse simulated attacker policies.

title: Microsoft AntiSSRF open-source library helps block server-side request forgery
blurb: Microsoft released AntiSSRF, an open-source MIT-licensed library for .NET and Node.js that validates URLs and outbound connections to block server-side request forgery before applications make requests. Configured via an AntiSSRFPolicy object, it supports IP allow/deny lists, denying unspecified addresses, blocking plain-text HTTP, and Azure Key Vault and Storage domain checks, with samples for HttpClient, Axios, follow-redirects, and node-fetch.

title: ARVO: Atlas of Reproducible Vulnerabilities for Open-Source Software
blurb: ARVO is a dataset of over 6,100 reproducible real-world vulnerabilities across 311 open-source projects, built by adding full reproducibility to OSS-Fuzz so each bug can be consistently rebuilt, triggered, and analyzed across versions. It reproduces 81% of vulnerabilities and locates the corresponding patch with 89.4% accuracy, enabling automated patch identification and post-fix analysis that prior large-scale datasets lack.

title: An Empirical Analysis of AI Slop in Music Streaming
blurb: A new paper analyzing AI-generated "slop" music on streaming platforms found that 93% of AI tracks receive few or no plays, while artists "spray and pray" by flooding multiple genres in hopes of a hit. The authors published their own AI songs through 11 indie distributors with inconsistent, largely unenforced AI policies, and warn that weak, non-robust detection methods will let slop become a self-sustaining shadow industry without industry intervention.

title: Security and Privacy Prompts in the Wild: What Users Ask LLMs and How LLMs Respond
blurb: A study mined WildChat's 3.2M user-LLM conversations to identify 14,727 security and privacy prompts, the first analysis of real-world S&P questions users pose to chatbots rather than expert-authored FAQs. Across 270 advice-seeking prompts run 10 times each, GPT-5.5 gave "good enough" answers 98% of the time versus Llama 4's 47%, but commercial models sometimes returned contradictory guidance across runs.

title: Cache to the Future: A Distributed Webpage Archive for Internet Blackouts
blurb: Researchers introduced Cache to the Future (CttF), a distributed system that caches and delivers static web content during internet blackouts caused by outages or government shutdowns. The design crowdsources caching through community ratings while using digital signatures and proofs-of-work to resist adversarial tampering, with simulations showing city-scale delivery under both benign and hostile conditions.

title: Multi-Source Cybersecurity Logs: An ATT&CK-Labeled Dataset and SLM Evaluation
blurb: Researchers released a multi-source cybersecurity log dataset of 870 sessions and roughly 2.3M events, capturing simultaneous Windows system, network, and browser telemetry with per-entry MITRE ATT&CK technique labels across 12 tactics and 53 techniques. Fine-tuning three SLMs with LoRA lifted chunk-classification accuracy from about 8% to 90–97%, though exact-match ATT&CK technique identification topped out at 42%.

title: ARES: A Platform for Adaptive Role-Based Evaluation of Social Engineering Risks in Human--AI Games
blurb: ARES, an open platform and 340 GB pilot dataset, audits adaptive social-engineering risks of role-conditioned LLM agents across human and AI social games.

title: Verifiable computations for dynamic encrypted control
blurb: A new verification method for linear dynamic encrypted control uses challenge signals to catch tampered cloud computations with negligible overhead and no replay risk.

title: Security-Induced Braess Paradoxes in Service Function Chain Orchestration
blurb: Adding defensive options to NFV/SDN service chains can backfire, raising cost 27-31% by concentrating traffic on shared security resources.

title: HSBC Expects Over $100 Million Gains From Using Google AI Tools
blurb: HSBC will deploy Google Cloud's AI tools across its global operations, with individual projects each expected to generate over $100 million in added revenue or cost savings. The partnership gives Google Cloud a flagship financial-services reference account and signals that large banks are moving AI spend from pilots into production-scale rollouts tied to concrete P&L targets.

title: Microsoft says you don’t need another email security tool; experts say, not so fast
blurb: Microsoft's new quarterly benchmark claims Defender for Office 365 catches the vast majority of malicious email on its own, missing 59% fewer high-severity threats than rival secure email gateways and gaining under 0.05% from integrated third-party partners. Security experts counter that aggregate percentages hide the absolute volume and severity of what slips through, and that one missed message can cause an incident, preserving the case for defense-in-depth. The pitch effectively pressures buyers to consolidate email security onto a single vendor.

title: “성능 향상 vs 데이터 우려”…스페이스X의 커서 인수에 엇갈린 평가
blurb: SpaceX formally announced an all-stock acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor for $60B, with a $10B breakup fee if the deal collapses, positioning xAI's GPU capacity to lift the compute bottleneck that analysts call Cursor's biggest constraint on growth. The deal raises governance questions for the 64% of Fortune 500 firms running Cursor, as CIOs weigh whether the tool keeps model choice beyond Grok and how xAI's guardrails fit enterprise data requirements.

title: The checklist problem behind critical infrastructure cyber safety
blurb: New research from George Mason University argues that US critical-infrastructure cyber policy has let compliance stand in for safety, governing operational technology with the same data-centric controls as IT. The paper documents cases where compliant controls created physical hazards: fail-secure door latches that trapped occupants during fires, account lockouts that denied emergency access, and patches that forced unplanned SCADA downtime. The implication for buyers is that audit checklists do not certify that equipment can withstand an attack or failure.

title: SNAS: A Multi-Layer Defense-in-Depth Architecture for Secure Egress in Sandboxed Workloads
blurb: Snowflake engineers detail SNAS, the production architecture that gives Snowpark's sandboxed UDFs external network access while preserving multi-tenant isolation. The design layers eBPF packet filtering, GENEVE overlay networks, and distributed egress proxies, plus an EDT-based eBPF bandwidth limiter and dual-tier policy enforcement to handle connection limits and port exhaustion. It runs across all Snowflake regions, supporting petabyte-scale transfers and latency-sensitive integrations.

title: Threads reaches 500 million user milestone
blurb: Threads reached 500 million monthly active users, closing the gap with X as Meta added Communities and new algorithm controls that let users tune their feeds. The growth gives marketers a maturing platform with first-party Meta ad integration and an engaged audience, positioning Threads as a credible organic and paid channel alongside X for brand reach.

title: Illinois Governor JB Pritzker signs a state budget that levies taxes on social media giants, digital advertising, and prediction and crypto markets (Mitchell Armentrout/Chicago Sun Times)
blurb: Illinois Governor JB Pritzker signed a state budget that imposes new taxes on social media platforms, digital advertising, and prediction and crypto markets. The digital-ad levy puts Illinois alongside Maryland in taxing ad spend at the state level, raising costs and compliance questions for marketers running Meta, Google, and programmatic campaigns in the state.

title: Kapwing: in tests, ~60% of the first 500 TikTok For You videos shown to a new account were AI slop, ~3x the slop shown to a new YouTube user via YouTube Shorts (Matt G. Southern/Search Engine Journal)
blurb: New TikTok accounts were served AI-generated "slop" in roughly 60% of their first 500 For You videos in Kapwing's tests, about 3x the rate a new YouTube user encounters on Shorts. The volume signals how fast generative content is saturating TikTok's feed, raising the bar for brands trying to stand out and the cost of looking native rather than synthetic.

title: Sources: Amazon faces a possible FTC lawsuit alleging it misled advertisers; multiple state AGs are involved and the case could seek billions in civil penalties (Bloomberg)
blurb: The FTC is preparing a possible lawsuit alleging Amazon misled advertisers, with multiple state attorneys general joining a case that could seek billions in civil penalties, according to Bloomberg. For brands buying Amazon Ads, the scrutiny raises pressure on the platform to clarify measurement and placement practices that advertisers rely on to justify retail-media spend.

title: Talking to Your Data: Exploring Embodied Conversation as an Interface for Personal Health Reflection
blurb: A research prototype replaces wearable-data dashboards with an embodied conversational agent that narrates your health stats aloud to prompt active reflection.

title: Trump-backed World Liberty Financial nears OCC approval for federal trust charter: report
blurb: World Liberty Financial, the Trump-family-backed crypto venture, is nearing OCC approval for a federal trust bank charter, according to a report. The charter would let World Liberty issue and redeem its USD1 stablecoin under a single federal regulator rather than navigating state-by-state money transmitter licensing.

title: Gatling: Rapid-Fire Consensus from Parallel Composition
blurb: Gatling is an atomic broadcast protocol that pushes inter-proposal time below one network delay, a limit prior consensus designs treated as the floor. It runs multiple parallel instances of a black-box BFT protocol on staggered proposal schedules, then merges their outputs into one totally ordered log via a deterministic interleaving rule. The paper derives the optimal number of parallel instances under head-of-line blocking from crashed leaders and shows the approach works with off-the-shelf component protocols without latency-tuning each one.

title: IsabeLLM: Automated Theorem Proving Applied to Formally Verifying Consensus
blurb: This paper extends IsabeLLM, an LLM-driven automated theorem prover built on Isabelle, to formally verify blockchain consensus protocols. The authors add a retrieval-augmented generation framework, error tracing, and counterexample generation to supply the model with better context, plus compatibility with current Isabelle and Sledgehammer versions. They benchmark the upgraded tool against the original on verifying Bitcoin's Proof of Work consensus, targeting the protocol layer where exploits cause the largest losses.

title: A look at Wyoming's Frontier Stable Token, which launched in January and currently has a market value of ~$1M, as other states explore "white labeling" the tech (David Voreacos/Bloomberg)
blurb: Wyoming's Frontier Stable Token, launched in January as the first US state-issued stablecoin, currently holds a market value of roughly $1M. Other states are now exploring "white labeling" the underlying technology, signaling a path for government-backed stablecoins to scale without each issuer building infrastructure from scratch.

title: Crypto PAC's $12 million Senate candidate, Barry Moore, wins Alabama GOP primary
blurb: Crypto super PAC Fairshake spent more than $12 million backing Representative Barry Moore, who won Alabama's Republican Senate primary runoff with nearly 56% of the vote, its largest single outlay this cycle. The result, in a deeply GOP state that favors Moore in November, offsets Fairshake's recent loss in Illinois and leaves the PAC network with roughly $150 million in cash on hand.

title: A look at Wyoming's Frontier Stable Token, which launched in January and currently has a market value of ~$1M, as other states explore "white labeling" the tech (David Voreacos/Bloomberg)
blurb: Wyoming's Frontier Stable Token (FRNT), the first stablecoin issued by a US state, launched in January and now carries a market value of roughly $1M. Other states are exploring "white labeling" Wyoming's underlying tech to issue their own tokens, suggesting state-level public stablecoins could proliferate even as federal frameworks like the GENIUS Act take shape.

title: HSBC Expects Over $100 Million Gains From Using Google AI Tools
blurb: HSBC will deploy Google Cloud AI across its global operations, with individual projects each expected to generate over $100 million in added revenue or cost savings. The bank's commitment signals how large incumbents are betting on hyperscaler AI partnerships to wring efficiency from sprawling operations, intensifying competition among cloud providers for financial-services workloads.

title: PBOC Launches Tool to Boost Yuan Use by Other Central Banks
blurb: The PBOC launched new money-market tools designed to expand offshore yuan usage among foreign central banks and sovereign wealth funds. The measures aim to deepen renminbi liquidity for official-sector holders, part of Beijing's long-running push to position the yuan as a reserve and settlement currency against dollar dominance.

title: Trump-backed World Liberty Financial nears OCC approval for federal trust charter: report
blurb: World Liberty Financial, the crypto venture backed by President Trump's family, is nearing OCC approval for a federal trust bank charter, according to a report. The charter would let World Liberty issue and redeem its USD1 stablecoin under a single federal regulator, removing the need to navigate state-by-state money-transmitter licensing.

title: China Watchdog Urges More IPOs From AI, Hong Kong Listed Firms
blurb: China's securities regulator, the CSRC, pledged to welcome more domestic listings from AI developers and Hong Kong-traded firms to deepen onshore capital markets. The push aims to keep high-growth tech companies listing at home rather than overseas, signaling Beijing wants its strategic AI sector funded through mainland exchanges as US listings stay constrained.

title: An AI Security Agent for Banking: Multi-Vector Fraud and AML Detection Across Retail and Corporate Accounts
blurb: This paper presents an AI security agent for retail and corporate banking that runs parallel transaction and session streams, each fusing an LSTM behavioral model, a velocity monitor, and a graph module tracking fan-in/fan-out and pass-through ratios for AML detection. On a synthetic log of 237,669 transactions across 13 threat categories, it hit F1 of 0.787 and 0.867, beating rule-based (0.562/0.733) and LSTM-only baselines, with Critical-tier response under 0.43 ms.
